scenarioName: Cardiogenic Shock — Anterior STEMI with Pulmonary Edema, Cautious Fluids, Pressors, CPAP

scenarioStory.history: 68-year-old retired warehouse foreman developed substernal chest pressure with diaphoresis approximately 4 hours ago while watching a basketball game with a beer. He attributed it to indigestion, took two Tums, and 'rode it out' alone in the recliner. Over the next 3 hours he became progressively more short of breath, started sweating heavily, and felt 'like the room was closing in.' His adult son stopped by to check on him, found him pale, gray, and gasping, and called 911. The patient now describes substernal pressure 'like a brick on my chest' rated 8/10, radiating to his left arm and jaw, with severe shortness of breath that prevents him from finishing a sentence. He has 30+ pack-year smoking history (still a half-pack per day), hypertension, type 2 diabetes, hyperlipidemia, and a prior NSTEMI 6 years ago treated medically (no stent placed at that time, on aspirin and statin since). Denies sildenafil/tadalafil use. Denies recent dental work or fevers.
scenarioStory.course: Initial Contact: EMS finds a 68-year-old man in obvious cardiogenic shock: pale, diaphoretic, cool extremities, sitting forward with frothy pink sputum, and unable to speak more than 1–2 words. Initial monitor shows sinus tachycardia at 118 with ST elevation in V1–V4 — anterior STEMI. BP is 78/52, lungs reveal rales to the mid-fields, and SpO2 is 86% on room air. This is cardiogenic shock with pulmonary edema — the protocol pathway is FUNDAMENTALLY DIFFERENT from straight septic or hypovolemic shock: a SMALL test fluid bolus (250 mL), early vasopressor support, CPAP for the pulmonary edema, and STEMI activation. Aggressive crystalloid here will worsen pulmonary edema. Nitrates are CONTRAINDICATED with this BP. The teaching point is recognition of the pump failure pattern + restraint with fluids + early pressor. → Cautious bolus + pressor + CPAP: Crew gives a small 250 mL NS test bolus while preparing norepinephrine. Norepinephrine drip started at 5 mcg/min (within 0.05–0.5 mcg/kg/min range — for 90 kg, 0.06 mcg/kg/min ≈ 5 mcg/min) titrated to MAP >65. CPAP applied at 5–10 cm H2O with high-flow O2. Within 5 minutes BP rises to 96/64 and SpO2 to 94%; rales recede slightly with CPAP. Patient remains in pain but mentation improves and he is now speaking in short phrases. STEMI alert active; transport to PCI-capable center underway. → Stable on pressor + CPAP; transport to cath lab: Patient transported to PCI-capable hospital with norepinephrine drip titrated and CPAP continued. Reassessing perfusion, MAP, oxygenation, and rhythm en route. Watching for VT/VF (anterior MI risk), hypotension breakthrough, or pulmonary edema rebound. Bedside handoff to interventional cardiology team includes door-to-EKG time, time of STEMI activation, all medications and drips, and the patient's anticipatory pain control discussion (opiates withheld so far due to marginal BP).

patientInformation.patientCondition: Severe shortness of breath, cold sweat, and crushing chest pressure that began about 4 hours ago and has progressively worsened; he sat through it 'thinking it was indigestion' until his adult son visited and called 911.

scenarioEvents.name: Initial Contact
scenarioEvents.description: EMS finds a 68-year-old man in obvious cardiogenic shock: pale, diaphoretic, cool extremities, sitting forward with frothy pink sputum, and unable to speak more than 1–2 words. Initial monitor shows sinus tachycardia at 118 with ST elevation in V1–V4 — anterior STEMI. BP is 78/52, lungs reveal rales to the mid-fields, and SpO2 is 86% on room air. This is cardiogenic shock with pulmonary edema — the protocol pathway is FUNDAMENTALLY DIFFERENT from straight septic or hypovolemic shock: a SMALL test fluid bolus (250 mL), early vasopressor support, CPAP for the pulmonary edema, and STEMI activation. Aggressive crystalloid here will worsen pulmonary edema. Nitrates are CONTRAINDICATED with this BP. The teaching point is recognition of the pump failure pattern + restraint with fluids + early pressor.
relatedChecklist: Recognize cardiogenic shock: cool/mottled, hypotension, AMS-trending, pulm edema, ↓EtCO2
12-lead immediately; identify anterior STEMI; activate STEMI alert with EKG transmission
Aspirin 324 mg PO chewed (4× 81 mg) — non-enteric coated
Withhold nitroglycerin — SBP 78 too low; document hold reason
IV access ×2 large-bore; cardiac monitor; SpO2 + EtCO2; defib pads on
Check blood glucose — rule out reversible cause
Prepare CPAP for pulmonary edema; titrate after small fluid + pressor stabilize BP

scenarioEvents.name: Cautious bolus + pressor + CPAP
scenarioEvents.description: Crew gives a small 250 mL NS test bolus while preparing norepinephrine. Norepinephrine drip started at 5 mcg/min (within 0.05–0.5 mcg/kg/min range — for 90 kg, 0.06 mcg/kg/min ≈ 5 mcg/min) titrated to MAP >65. CPAP applied at 5–10 cm H2O with high-flow O2. Within 5 minutes BP rises to 96/64 and SpO2 to 94%; rales recede slightly with CPAP. Patient remains in pain but mentation improves and he is now speaking in short phrases. STEMI alert active; transport to PCI-capable center underway.
relatedChecklist: 250 mL NS test bolus only — STOP if rales worsen (cardiogenic-shock fluid rule)
Norepinephrine 5 mcg/min IV (90 kg → ~0.06 mcg/kg/min) titrate to MAP >65
Push-dose epi 10 mcg IV ready (1 mL of 10 mcg/mL dilution) if BP collapses while drip runs
CPAP 5→10 cm H2O with high FiO2; titrate to SpO2 94–98% and reduced WOB
STEMI alert; transport directly to PCI-capable hospital; serial 12-leads en route

scenarioEvents.name: Stable on pressor + CPAP; transport to cath lab
scenarioEvents.description: Patient transported to PCI-capable hospital with norepinephrine drip titrated and CPAP continued. Reassessing perfusion, MAP, oxygenation, and rhythm en route. Watching for VT/VF (anterior MI risk), hypotension breakthrough, or pulmonary edema rebound. Bedside handoff to interventional cardiology team includes door-to-EKG time, time of STEMI activation, all medications and drips, and the patient's anticipatory pain control discussion (opiates withheld so far due to marginal BP).
relatedChecklist: Continuous monitor for VT/VF; pads stay on; defib mode ready
Reassess vitals + neuro + lung exam q5 min; titrate norepi to MAP >65
Update receiving hospital with vitals, dose timeline, and ETA; handoff at bedside with strips

scenarioEvents.name: Aggressive 2 L bolus given
scenarioEvents.description: Crew anchored on a generic 'shock = fluids' approach and pressure-infused 2 L of NS without recognizing the cardiogenic pattern (cool/mottled, rales, frothy sputum, anterior STEMI). Within minutes the patient's pulmonary edema worsens dramatically — SpO2 falls into the 70s, rales rise to the apices, frothy pink sputum increases, and BP actually FALLS as the failing left ventricle decompensates further. The MATC Shock protocol explicitly states: 'If the patient condition deteriorates after fluid administration, rales or hepatomegaly develop, then consider cardiogenic shock and withholding further fluid administration.' The crew must STOP fluids immediately, escalate to CPAP/BVM with PEEP, start a vasopressor, and prepare for possible airway intervention if respiratory failure progresses.
relatedChecklist: STOP IV fluids; recognize deterioration after bolus = cardiogenic shock
BVM with PEEP valve (or CPAP if tolerated); suction frothy sputum; FiO2 100%
Start norepinephrine 5–10 mcg/min (≈0.06–0.11 mcg/kg/min for 90 kg) titrate to MAP >65
Push-dose epi 10 mcg IV as bridge while drip is prepared
STEMI alert remains active; consider divert if respiratory failure progresses
